description: Thursday, 19 June 2025 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Business, Science and Innovation (excluding appropriations related to the Health Research Fund and New Zealand Trade and Enterprise) Hearing of evidence: Minister of Commerce and Consumer Affairs Hon Scott Simpson Ministry of Business, Innovation, and Employment Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Building, Resources and Markets Andrew Hume, General Manager Commerce, Consumer and Business Jenna Reid, Policy Director Alistair Dixon, Policy Director Sanjai Raj, General Manager Market Integrity Ingrid Bayliss, General Manager Building and Tenancy Stephanie Greathead, Head of Accommodation and Insurance Response

chunks.text: Right item Bushy home At the end of a long day. And you're the last, last item of business for this committee. So you gotta bring the fun. Um, yeah. Sorry I didn't bring sugar or something. Yeah. For you, sort of per you up a bit. Yeah. Worry. But It has been a long day.

I'm very aware of that. Yeah. Thank you, minister. So, um, your team is settled. Uh, we have, uh, one hour with you. So if you would like to start by some opening remarks, uh, keeping up for around four minutes, that would be good. Yeah. Look, Uh, thank you very much, and chair, thank you for the opportunity to be with you this afternoon.

I'm, and I'm very conscious that you've had, uh, a, a long day. Um, look, I've been, um, uh, privileged to be the minister for a little less than four months. So it's fair to say that it's been a relatively steep learning curve. And you'll be aware of the, uh, um, work program that, uh, I inherited from my predecessor.

So I should just say I've got, uh, Paul and Carolyn here from, uh, the, from MB that are here supporting me. And also there are, um, team members, uh, behind as well. So we'll see how we go in terms of answering, uh, questions. But look, the, uh, commerce and consumer affairs portfolio is a really important portfolio.

Uh, at its essence, we are all consumers. We are all members of the, uh, economy, the ecosystem. Every time we go and buy a cup of coffee in ACON at a cafe, we are consumers. We are engaged. And so the work that is done in the portfolio can often seem to those of us that, um, may have been on the outside of it, some of that detailed work can sometimes sound a bit dry and a bit, um, uh, precise.

Uh, and sometimes the language that is used in terms of the legislation and also the jargon of the various sectors can also be, um, a little bit, uh, sometimes dry to those people who aren't familiar with it. So, um, one of the key concerns for me in this role is actually, um, the, um, uh, focus on what's good for consumers.

How do we make the regulatory, uh, legislative, uh, and e economic, uh, drivers that, uh, impact us all every day? How can we make those easier, more efficient, um, and, uh, providing better outcomes for New Zealanders? So, I want to just mention a couple of the key areas that are currently on my work program, uh, and then I'll be very happy to take, uh, questions from you.

So there are, um, a number of key things that we've, uh, progressed. Uh, one of them is the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act. Um, the streamlining of, uh, conduct of financial institutions. Uh, and some of these changes are designed to remove unnecessary regulation and ensure adequate protections for our most, uh, vulnerable consumers.

So, uh, there are bills currently before the select committee now, and, um, they include the contracts of insurance, the customer and data products that has actually already been passed. Uh, that's the, um, uh, so-called open banking legislation. Um, I've just had a meeting with, uh, uh, a FinTech company that is really looking forward to the opportunities that will be available to New Zealanders, uh, shortly, hopefully, um, uh, just before Christmas, the big banks are gonna have to be ready for that.

The smaller second tier banks, uh, get a, an opportunity to be a little bit later. That's more to do with resourcing than anything else. The bigger banks, uh, have literally more resource available to them. But I'm very excited about that, uh, opportunity. And unless New Zealanders have actually lived overseas or traveled overseas and seen the positive impact, um, of various fintechs, I really won't have an understanding or an idea of what it all means until they kind of see it in and it's kind of real, uh, and it has meaning in their, in their lives.

Um, the other area that I want to talk about just briefly at the moment is, um, my role in this portfolio, in terms of the government's lead, uh, minister on antis scam activity. I have to be careful to put the ante in front of it. I've been a couple of occasions when I've been introduced as the Minister for Scams.

Um, and, um, that's, uh, a little bit problematic. So, um, we just have to get the, uh, the jargon right on that. But, um, we all know that too many New Zealanders are being scammed. The quantum, um, is hard to actually, uh, define, but, uh, at its very minimum, it runs into hundreds of millions of dollars a year at its upper level.

It could be as high as $2 billion a year. Uh, and we are all susceptible to it. Um, and so I'm hoping that someone will ask me a question about that, because I don't want to spend too much time talking about it, um, uh, right now in my introductory comments. Uh, but there is still much work to do. Uh, I'm very grateful for the work that, um, MB officials are doing in terms of a whole range of, of work.

And one of the things that I have been immediately impressed by in the short time that I have been in this ministerial role, is the capable professional, uh, and, and at pace ability of MB officials to work in areas that are actually quite complicated, quite technical, and also areas that, um, have, uh, an impact literally on the day-to-day lives of all New Zealanders.

Even if those New Zealanders don't necessarily, uh, understand, uh, how that work impacts on them. They feel it and see it, uh, as I say, when they're, when they're there. So, look, um, uh, the other piece of work I just wanted to mention is, um, the company's act, uh, there's a, a company's act has not been reviewed for a long number of years.

It's largely a piece of legislation that was based on an analog, uh, view of the world. Um, and, uh, as you know, we now live in a very digitized world. And so we need to update, upgrade and modernize our companies at. So, look, that's just a bit of a snapshot. Um, Madam Chair, I'm very happy to, uh, take questions and I will be ablely assisted by, uh, colleagues should the need arise.

So, thank you, Madam Chair. Thank You. Thank you, minister. We'll start with Ena Williams, Madam Chair. Thank you. I have, um, a primary and then three steps to this question. That's right. Um, and so the 2019 position is the existing law for matters that have occurred post 2019, but there are some situations, um, current and also potential that occurred between 2015 and 2019, and it's that aspect that, um, the legislation seeks to address.

That's why it's, that's the purpose of, of a parliamentary parliamentary process to tease out these, these issues. Uh, I think these are issues that should be tested.

I agree. Thank you. And grateful for that commitment to the full, um, select committee process, which it's going on now. Um, n no, that's not the purpose of this proposed legislative change. Well, I, I received a wide range of advice and on balance, I think this is a matter that is worth testing, uh, in the parliament through select committee process,

through proposed legislation. Okay. So, uh, I have a supplementary form, Dr. Thank you, um, Madam Chair. but at the same time, uh, that they're not gonna get bogged down as well. but not have what we actually saw, which was a, uh, a freezing of the, the con of the, the lending market. So what we found that we were having unintended consequences, uh, as a result of the Triple CFA over the years. I think the changes, uh, a couple of, uh, things about the changes, uh, the movement to the FMA is intended to make it a lot clearer about which regulator is responsible for financial regulations. And the FMA and ComCom Commerce Commission takes slightly different approaches to their regulatory activities.

Uh, the Commerce Commission, um, is in, I suppose in some ways a more traditional regulator where they think about compliance and enforcement and legal cases. The financial markets authority does that as well, but they also take quite a strong focus on, uh, education and working alongside the industry, uh, which is something that the Commerce Commission, it's a little harder for them to do.

So in some ways, moving this to the, to the fmma is intended to both, um, ensure that there's, there's a strong regulatory, uh, compliance toolkit that is applied by a strong regulator, but is also working with all the financial institutions across the range of inventions that they do to lift their game. Um, uh, the telco companies that, uh, have the ability to, uh, facilitate the sending of the, hi mum, I've just got a new cell phone message now that comes through a telco.

So we've already seen as a result of that, that the banks have come out with a new code of practice, which, uh, gives New Zealand, uh, customers, bank customers a higher level of protection, um, and sets out a series of protocols, uh, that should a person be scammed and the bank has been at fault.

It creates, uh, uh, a range of compensatory, uh, uh, rules that are available to them. I want to now extend that beyond the banks to, uh, the next one would be something similar from the telcos. And then, um, at very least of the digital platform providers are a little bit more tricky 'cause they're a bit more diverse.

They're not quite as, um, um, uh, uh, um, uh, I was gonna say organized, that's the wrong word. Um, so, um, what I would hope from the digital platform providers, and what I've made it very clear I want from them is a level of support and, um, uh, commitment that they will provide New Zealanders with no less than is being provided in Australia, for instance.

Now, um, uh, all this is being achieved this effectively, this partnership between these, these organizations, uh, in a way that, um, hasn't cost the New Zealand taxpayer any money. We haven't had to legislate, we haven't had to regulate. And yet we are on the cusp of achieving, uh, uh, a very good outcome for New Zealand customers in a way that I think is, um, quite exciting and will ultimately lead to a safer, um, antis scam environment for them.

But scammers are clever, they're well resourced, they are increasingly, um, international and to a degree, um, there is an element of sort of whack-a-mole about, um, scams. You sort of have to, you know, no sooner do you sort of close one thing off, and then something else will, will, um, pop up.

So I don't think we get to a point where we can ever sort of rest on our laurels and say, job done, everything's fixed. It's, you know, it's now perfect. That said, there is an element of personal responsibility, uh, that is at play here.

Mm-hmm. And New Zealanders need, in my view, to be more alert, more aware, more conscious, and sometimes far less trusting of, uh, uh, approaches that they have.
